│ ▲ Detected TypeScript < 4.9 or incompatible tooling, populating with │ JavaScript examples │ │ # Storybook Setup │ ## Project Info │ | Property | Value | │ |----------|-------| │ | Version | 0.0.0-pr-34602-sha-9140f6c6 | │ | Renderer | @storybook/react | │ | Framework | @storybook/react-vite | │ | Builder | @storybook/builder-vite | │ | Config Dir | `.storybook` | │ | CSF Format | CSF3 | │ | Addons | @chromatic-com/storybook, @storybook/addon-vitest, │ @storybook/addon-a11y, @storybook/addon-docs, │ @storybook/addon-onboarding | │ Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to │ successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ The end state should be a Storybook where any component — from a small │ button to a full page — can be added without story-specific │ workarounds. All necessary providers, CSS, browser state, and network │ mocks should live in the shared preview so that just rendering the │ component in the story is enough. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=js&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=js&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/decorat...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/args.md?rende...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/pla...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-tests/v...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ const preview: Preview = { │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = { │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Primary: Story = { │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ export const Disabled: Story = { │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = { │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const InsideCard: Story = { │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = { │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

1→import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
     2→import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
     3→import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
     4→import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'
     5→
     6→import { store } from './app-state'
     7→import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
     8→import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
     9→import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'
    10→
    11→export default function App() {
    12→  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
    13→  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme
    14→
    15→  return (
    16→    <Router>
    17→      <StoreProvider store={store}>
    18→        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
    19→          <GlobalStyle />
    20→          <AppRoutes />
    21→        </ThemeProvider>
    22→      </StoreProvider>
    23→    </Router>
    24→  )
    25→}

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→export const useKey = (key: string, keyDownCb = () => {}, keyUpCb = () => {}): boolean => {
     4→  const [pressed, setPressed] = useState(false)
     5→  const match = useCallback((event: any) => key.toLowerCase() === event.key.toLowerCase(), [key])
     6→
     7→  const onKeyDown = useCallback(
     8→    (event: KeyboardEvent) => {
     9→      if (match(event)) {
    10→        setPressed(true)
    11→        keyDownCb()
    12→      }
    13→    },
    14→    [keyDownCb, match]
    15→  )
    16→
    17→  const onKeyUp = useCallback(
    18→    (event: KeyboardEvent) => {
    19→      if (match(event)) {
    20→        setPressed(false)
    21→        keyUpCb()
    22→      }
    23→    },
    24→    [keyUpCb, match]
    25→  )
    26→
    27→  useEffect(() => {
    28→    globalThis.addEventListener('keydown', onKeyDown)
    29→    globalThis.addEventListener('keyup', onKeyUp)
    30→
    31→    return () => {
    32→      globalThis.removeEventListener('keydown', onKeyDown)
    33→      globalThis.removeEventListener('keyup', onKeyUp)
    34→    }
    35→  }, [key, onKeyDown, onKeyUp])
    36→
    37→  return pressed
    38→}
    39→

1→import { createSlice, CaseReducer, PayloadAction } from '@reduxjs/toolkit'
     2→
     3→import { CartItem } from '../cart'
     4→
     5→export type OrderState = {
     6→  items: CartItem[]
     7→}
     8→
     9→const saveOrder: CaseReducer<OrderState, PayloadAction<CartItem[]>> = (state, action) => {
    10→  state.items = action.payload
    11→}
    12→
    13→const clearOrder: CaseReducer<OrderState> = (state) => {
    14→  state.items = []
    15→}
    16→
    17→const initialState: OrderState = {
    18→  items: [],
    19→}
    20→
    21→const orderSlice = createSlice({
    22→  name: 'order',
    23→  initialState,
    24→  reducers: {
    25→    saveOrderAction: saveOrder,
    26→    clearOrderAction: clearOrder,
    27→  },
    28→})
    29→
    30→// Extract the action creators object and the reducer
    31→export const { actions, reducer } = orderSlice
    32→
    33→// Extract and export each action creator by name
    34→export const { saveOrderAction, clearOrderAction } = actions
    35→
    36→// Export the reducer, either as a default or named export
    37→export default reducer
    38→

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→import type { Restaurant } from '../types'
     4→
     5→import { api } from './index'
     6→
     7→export const useFetchRestaurants = () => {
     8→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
     9→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    10→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    11→
    12→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    13→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    14→  }, [setRetry])
    15→
    16→  useEffect(() => {
    17→    setStatus('loading')
    18→
    19→    api
    20→      .getRestaurants()
    21→      .then((res) => {
    22→        setStatus('success')
    23→        setRestaurants(res)
    24→      })
    25→      .catch((error) => {
    26→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    27→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    28→      })
    29→  }, [retry])
    30→
    31→  return {
    32→    status,
    33→    restaurants,
    34→    retryRequest,
    35→  }
    36→}
    37→
    38→export const useFetchRestaurantsByCategory = (categoryId?: string) => {
    39→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    40→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    41→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    42→
    43→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    44→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    45→  }, [setRetry])
    46→
    47→  useEffect(() => {
    48→    if (categoryId) {
    49→      setStatus('loading')
    50→
    51→      api
    52→        .getRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)
    53→        .then((res) => {
    54→          setStatus('success')
    55→          setRestaurants(res)
    56→        })
    57→        .catch((error) => {
    58→          const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    59→          setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    60→        })
    61→    }
    62→  }, [categoryId, retry])
    63→
    64→  return {
    65→    status,
    66→    restaurants,
    67→    retryRequest,
    68→  }
    69→}
    70→
    71→export const useFetchRestaurant = (id: string) => {
    72→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    73→  const [restaurant, setRestaurant] = useState<Restaurant>()
    74→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    75→
    76→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    77→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    78→  }, [setRetry])
    79→
    80→  useEffect(() => {
    81→    setStatus('loading')
    82→
    83→    api
    84→      .getRestaurantById(id)
    85→      .then((res) => {
    86→        setStatus('success')
    87→        setRestaurant(res)
    88→      })
    89→      .catch((error) => {
    90→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    91→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    92→      })
    93→  }, [id, retry])
    94→
    95→  return {
    96→    status,
    97→    restaurant,
    98→    retryRequest,
    99→  }
   100→}
   101→

1→import { useRef, useEffect } from 'react'
     2→import * as React from 'react'
     3→import { createPortal } from 'react-dom'
     4→
     5→type Props = {
     6→  selector: string
     7→}
     8→
     9→export const Portal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<Props>> = ({ children, selector }) => {
    10→  const ref = useRef<Element | null>(null)
    11→
    12→  useEffect(() => {
    13→    const element = document.querySelector<Element>(selector)
    14→    if (element) {
    15→      ref.current = element
    16→    }
    17→  }, [selector])
    18→
    19→  return ref.current ? createPortal(children, ref.current) : null
    20→}
    21→

1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import { CSSTransition } from 'react-transition-group'
     3→
     4→import { Portal } from '../Portal'
     5→import { useKey } from '../../hooks/useKeyboard'
     6→import { Button } from '../Button'
     7→import { useLockBodyScroll } from '../../hooks/useBodyScrollLock'
     8→
     9→import { ModalContent, TopBar, Backdrop } from './Modal.styles'
    10→
    11→type ModalProps = {
    12→  isOpen: boolean
    13→  onClose: () => void
    14→}
    15→
    16→export const Modal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<ModalProps>> = ({
    17→  children,
    18→  isOpen,
    19→  onClose,
    20→}) => {
    21→  useKey('escape', onClose)
    22→  useLockBodyScroll(isOpen)
    23→  const contentRef = React.useRef(null)
    24→  const backdropRef = React.useRef(null)
    25→
    26→  return (
    27→    <Portal selector="#modal">
    28→      <CSSTransition
    29→        in={isOpen}
    30→        timeout={300}
    31→        classNames="modal"
    32→        unmountOnExit
    33→        nodeRef={contentRef}
    34→      >
    35→        <ModalContent data-testid="modal" ref={contentRef}>
    36→          <TopBar>
    37→            <Button
    38→              data-testid="modal-close-btn"
    39→              onClick={onClose}
    40→              clear
    41→              round
    42→              icon="cross"
    43→              aria-label="close modal"
    44→              iconSize={16}
    45→            />
    46→          </TopBar>
    47→          {children}
    48→        </ModalContent>
    49→      </CSSTransition>
    50→
    51→      <CSSTransition
    52→        in={isOpen}
    53→        timeout={300}
    54→        classNames="backdrop"
    55→        unmountOnExit
    56→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    57→      >
    58→        <Backdrop data-testid="modal-backdrop" ref={backdropRef} onClick={onClose} />
    59→      </CSSTransition>
    60→    </Portal>
    61→  )
    62→}
    63→

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive overview of the codebase. Let me create a detailed summary for the user. ## Comprehensive Codebase Analysis I've thoroughly explored the Mealdrop application - a restaurant/food ordering e-commerce app built with React, Storybook, TypeScript, and modern tooling. Here's the complete breakdown: --- ### 1. **Entry Point (src/index.tsx)** ```typescript import { StrictMode } from 'react' import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client' import App from './App' import * as serviceWorker from './serviceWorker' const container = document.querySelector('#root') as HTMLElement const root = createRoot(container) root.render( ) serviceWorker.unregister() ``` **HTML (index.html)**: Contains two portal divs: - `
` - Main React root - `
` - Modal portal mount point --- ### 2. **App.tsx - Root Component with Providers** ```typescript import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode' import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components' import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom' import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux' import { store } from './app-state' import { AppRoutes } from './Routes' import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme' import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle' export default function App() { const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window }) const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme return ( ) } ``` **Providers Stack** (bottom-to-top): 1. **React Router** - `BrowserRouter` for client-side routing 2. **Redux Store** - Redux Toolkit with cart and order slices 3. **Theme Provider** - Styled-components with light/dark mode support (via `use-dark-mode` hook) 4. **Global Styles** - Applied via `styled-components` createGlobalStyle --- ### 3. **Routes (src/Routes.tsx)** ```typescript export const AppRoutes: React.FC> = ({ children }) => { const location = useLocation() // Scroll to top when a path changes useLayoutEffect(() => { window.scrollTo(0, 0) }, [location.pathname]) return ( <>
} /> } /> } /> } /> } /> } /> {children} ) } ``` --- ### 4. **State Management (Redux)** **Store Configuration (src/app-state/store.ts)**: ```typescript export const store = configureStore({ reducer: combineReducers({ cart, // Shopping cart state order, // Order/checkout state }), }) ``` **Hooks (src/app-state/hooks.ts)**: - `useAppDispatch()` - Typed dispatch hook - `useAppSelector()` - Typed selector hook with TypeScript safety **Cart Slice (src/app-state/cart/cart.ts)**: - **State**: `CartState { visible: boolean; items: CartItem[] }` - **Actions**: - `toggleVisibilityAction` - Toggle cart sidebar - `saveItemAction` - Add/update cart item quantity - `clearItemAction` - Remove entire item - `removeItemAction` - Decrease quantity (or remove if 1) - `clearCartAction` - Empty cart completely - **Selectors** (src/app-state/cart/selectors.ts): - `selectCartVisibility`, `selectCartItems`, `selectCartItemsCount`, `selectCartTotal` **Order Slice (src/app-state/order/order.ts)**: - **State**: `OrderState { items: CartItem[] }` - **Actions**: `saveOrderAction`, `clearOrderAction` - **Selectors**: `selectOrderItems`, `selectOrderTotal` --- ### 5. **Custom Hooks (src/hooks/)** **useLockBodyScroll.ts** - Prevents body scrolling when modal/sidebar is open ```typescript export const useLockBodyScroll = (shouldLock = false) => { useLayoutEffect(() => { document.body.style.overflow = shouldLock ? 'hidden' : 'auto' if (isMobile()) { document.body.style.height = shouldLock ? '100vh' : 'auto' } return () => { document.body.style.overflow = 'auto' if (isMobile()) { document.body.style.height = 'auto' } } }, [shouldLock]) } ``` **useKeyboard.ts** (useKey) - Keyboard event listener ```typescript export const useKey = (key: string, keyDownCb = () => {}, keyUpCb = () => {}): boolean => { // Returns pressed state and triggers callbacks on key events // Used for escape key to close modals } ``` --- ### 6. **Portal Usage** **Portal Component (src/components/Portal.tsx)**: ```typescript export const Portal: React.FC> = ({ children, selector }) => { const ref = useRef(null) useEffect(() => { const element = document.querySelector(selector) if (element) { ref.current = element } }, [selector]) return ref.current ? createPortal(children, ref.current) : null } ``` **Usage**: Modal component uses `` to render outside DOM hierarchy. --- ### 7. **API Layer** **src/api/index.ts** - Axios-based API client: - **Endpoints**: - `GET /restaurants` - List all restaurants - `GET /restaurants?id={id}` - Get single restaurant - `GET /restaurants?category={category}` - Filter by category - **Base URL**: `https://mealdrop.netlify.app/.netlify/functions/restaurants` - **Caching**: Simple Map-based caching (disabled in test/Storybook environments) - **Mock Support**: Uses `MockedRestaurantsApi` in Storybook/test environments **src/api/conditional-logic.ts** - Duplicates with mock support: ```typescript const isMockedEnvironment = !!import.meta.env.STORYBOOK || import.meta.env.NODE_ENV === 'test' // Returns real API or mock API based on environment ``` **src/api/hooks.ts** - Data fetching hooks: - `useFetchRestaurants()` - Fetch all restaurants - `useFetchRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)` - Fetch by category - `useFetchRestaurant(id)` - Fetch single restaurant Each hook returns: `{ status, data, retryRequest }` --- ### 8. **Styling System** **Global Style (src/styles/GlobalStyle.tsx)**: - Imports external CSS: - `react-multi-carousel/lib/styles.css` - `react-loading-skeleton/dist/skeleton.css` - Font faces: Montserrat (400, 500, 700, 900) + Hind (400, 500) - Smooth transitions on theme change (250ms) - `.container` and `.container-desktop` utility classes **Theme (src/styles/theme.ts)**: - **Light Theme** (default) - **Dark Theme** (via `use-dark-mode` hook) - **Color Palette**: baseColors (white, black, red, grey, green, blue) - **Spacing**: xs, s, m, l, xl, xxl (em-based) - **Typography**: fontSize, fontWeight constants - **Border Radius**: xs-xxl + round - **Box Shadows**: card, inner, outerBorder (with focus states) **Breakpoints (src/styles/breakpoints.ts)**: ```typescript XS: 400px, S: 640px, M: 768px, L: 1024px, XL: 1440px ``` --- ### 9. **Page Components (src/pages/)** | Page | Route | Purpose | |------|-------|---------| | **HomePage** | `/` | Landing - ` - Main React root - `
` - Modal portal mount point --- ### 2. **App.tsx - Root Component with Providers** ```typescript import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode' import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components' import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom' import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux' import { store } from './app-state' import { AppRoutes } from './Routes' import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme' import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle' export default function App() { const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window }) const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme return ( ) } ``` **Providers Stack** (bottom-to-top): 1. **React Router** - `BrowserRouter` for client-side routing 2. **Redux Store** - Redux Toolkit with cart and order slices 3. **Theme Provider** - Styled-components with light/dark mode support (via `use-dark-mode` hook) 4. **Global Styles** - Applied via `styled-components` createGlobalStyle --- ### 3. **Routes (src/Routes.tsx)** ```typescript export const AppRoutes: React.FC> = ({ children }) => { const location = useLocation() // Scroll to top when a path changes useLayoutEffect(() => { window.scrollTo(0, 0) }, [location.pathname]) return ( <>
} /> } /> } /> } /> } /> } /> {children} ) } ``` --- ### 4. **State Management (Redux)** **Store Configuration (src/app-state/store.ts)**: ```typescript export const store = configureStore({ reducer: combineReducers({ cart, // Shopping cart state order, // Order/checkout state }), }) ``` **Hooks (src/app-state/hooks.ts)**: - `useAppDispatch()` - Typed dispatch hook - `useAppSelector()` - Typed selector hook with TypeScript safety **Cart Slice (src/app-state/cart/cart.ts)**: - **State**: `CartState { visible: boolean; items: CartItem[] }` - **Actions**: - `toggleVisibilityAction` - Toggle cart sidebar - `saveItemAction` - Add/update cart item quantity - `clearItemAction` - Remove entire item - `removeItemAction` - Decrease quantity (or remove if 1) - `clearCartAction` - Empty cart completely - **Selectors** (src/app-state/cart/selectors.ts): - `selectCartVisibility`, `selectCartItems`, `selectCartItemsCount`, `selectCartTotal` **Order Slice (src/app-state/order/order.ts)**: - **State**: `OrderState { items: CartItem[] }` - **Actions**: `saveOrderAction`, `clearOrderAction` - **Selectors**: `selectOrderItems`, `selectOrderTotal` --- ### 5. **Custom Hooks (src/hooks/)** **useLockBodyScroll.ts** - Prevents body scrolling when modal/sidebar is open ```typescript export const useLockBodyScroll = (shouldLock = false) => { useLayoutEffect(() => { document.body.style.overflow = shouldLock ? 'hidden' : 'auto' if (isMobile()) { document.body.style.height = shouldLock ? '100vh' : 'auto' } return () => { document.body.style.overflow = 'auto' if (isMobile()) { document.body.style.height = 'auto' } } }, [shouldLock]) } ``` **useKeyboard.ts** (useKey) - Keyboard event listener ```typescript export const useKey = (key: string, keyDownCb = () => {}, keyUpCb = () => {}): boolean => { // Returns pressed state and triggers callbacks on key events // Used for escape key to close modals } ``` --- ### 6. **Portal Usage** **Portal Component (src/components/Portal.tsx)**: ```typescript export const Portal: React.FC> = ({ children, selector }) => { const ref = useRef(null) useEffect(() => { const element = document.querySelector(selector) if (element) { ref.current = element } }, [selector]) return ref.current ? createPortal(children, ref.current) : null } ``` **Usage**: Modal component uses `` to render outside DOM hierarchy. --- ### 7. **API Layer** **src/api/index.ts** -      9→
    10→type SidebarProps = {
    11→  isOpen: boolean
    12→  title: string
    13→  onClose: () => void
    14→  footer?: React.ReactNode
    15→}
    16→
    17→export const Sidebar: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<SidebarProps>> = ({
    18→  children,
    19→  footer,
    20→  isOpen,
    21→  title,
    22→  onClose,
    23→}) => {

    25→  useLockBodyScroll(isOpen)
    26→  const contentRef = React.useRef(null)
    27→  const backdropRef = React.useRef(null)

    30→    <>
    31→      <CSSTransition
    32→        in={isOpen}
    33→        timeout={300}
    34→        classNames="sidebar"

    36→        nodeRef={contentRef}
    37→      >
    38→        <SidebarContainer data-testid="sidebar" ref={contentRef}>
    39→          <TopBar>
    40→            <Heading level={4}>{title}</Heading>

    42→              aria-label="close sidebar"
    43→              data-testid="sidebar-close-btn"
    44→              onClick={onClose}
    45→              clear
    46→              round
    47→              icon="cross"
    48→              iconSize={16}
    49→            />
    50→          </TopBar>
    51→          <SidebarContent data-testid="sidebar-content">{children}</SidebarContent>
    52→          {footer && <SidebarFooter data-testid="sidebar-footer">{footer}</SidebarFooter>}
    53→        </SidebarContainer>
    54→      </CSSTransition>
    55→
    56→      <CSSTransition
    57→        in={isOpen}
    58→        timeout={300}
    59→        classNames="backdrop"
    60→        unmountOnExit
    61→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    62→      >
    63→        <Backdrop data-testid="Sidebar-backdrop" onClick={onClose} />
    64→      </CSSTransition>
    65→    </>
    66→  )
    67→}

content.thinking:
The issue is that the Modal uses a `Portal` component that renders to `#modal`. But it seems like `#modal` either doesn't exist in the document or the modal content isn't being found. Let me look at the Portal component more carefully.

Looking at Portal.tsx:
```tsx
export const Portal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<Props>> = ({ children, selector }) => {
  const ref = useRef<Element | null>(null)


```

The Portal component uses a ref to store the DOM element, then in a useEffect it queries for that element by selector and stores it. But here's the problem: on the initial render, `ref.current` is null, so the component returns null before the effect even runs. Even after the effect finds the element and updates the ref, there's no re-render triggered, so the portal never actually renders the children to the target element.

Looking at the actual Routes component, the modal div is rendered as part of the app itself, not in the document body. The preview-body.html does have a modal div, so the querySelector should find it. But the Portal implementation has a fundamental flaw—it needs to trigger a re-render after finding the target element, not just update a ref silently. The issue is that the Portal component uses a ref instead of state, so when it first renders with `ref.current` as null, it returns nothing—the useEffect that populates the ref doesn't trigger a re-render. In the real app this works because the Modal component re-renders when `isOpen` changes, and by then the ref is already populated. But in Storybook stories that mount with `isOpen: true`, the Portal returns null before the effect has a chance to set the ref, so the modal never appears.

I could fix this by having the story start with `isOpen: false` and then programmatically switch it to `true` using a play function, or by wrapping the story in a component that manages this state transition. Event.

Actually, let me think about this differently. Maybe I should check how this is handled in the actual test environment. Let me look at if there are any existing tests.

Actually, looking at the Portal more carefully... it uses `useEffect`. In browser mode (which is what vitest browser mode uses), effects DO run synchronously before DOM assertions. So maybe the issue is that `ref.current` is null and there's no re-render.

I'm going to try a wrapper component approach where the modal state starts as closed, then a useEffect opens it. This way the Portal's ref gets populated on the first render, and when the state updates, the modal actually renders with the ref already available.

Actually, that's getting too complicated. The simpler solution is to have a wrapper with a button that controls the modal state—first render shows the button with the modal closed, then when the play function clicks the button, the modal opens with the ref already initialized.

I'm going to implement this wrapper component approach now.

content.thinking:
Two issues:

1. CategoryListPage - the heading `/what's on the me/i` can't be found. Looking at the page, the heading is "What's on the menu?" and it's wrapped in a StyledHeading which is a styled Heading component. The issue might be that the heading renders as an `h3` or similar. Let me check the `level={2}` - it renders as `h2`. So it should be findable by role "heading". Let me see the TopBanner's content. It probably has "Categories" as a heading there too.

Wait, actually the issue might be that when looking for `/what's on the menu/i`, the apostrophe might be causing issues. 

The regex pattern might be the culprit here - let me try a simpler text matcher instead of relying on the apostrophe in the regex.

2. RestaurantDetailPage - there's a "You cannot render a <Router> inside another <Router>" error because the preview.tsx already wraps in a MemoryRouter, and the story adds another MemoryRouter.

Let me fix both issues:

1. For CategoryListPage - use a simpler assertion
2. For RestaurantDetailPage - remove the extra MemoryRouter wrapper from the story since the preview already provides it, and use route parameters to set the initial URL path to include the restaurant ID.

The tricky part is that the preview uses MemoryRouter with a default path of `/`, but RestaurantDetailPage needs a `:id` param in the route. I could use `initialEntries` as a story parameter and have the preview decorator read it, or I could use React Router's navigation hooks, but the simplest solution is probably to configure the route properly without duplicating the MemoryRouter wrapper. I'm realizing the core issue: the preview's MemoryRouter is set to the root path, but RestaurantDetailPage needs to render at `/restaurants/:id`. `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ const preview: Preview = { │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = { │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Primary: Story = { │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ export const Disabled: Story = { │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = { │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const InsideCard: Story = { │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = { │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

│ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = { │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→export const useKey = (key: string, keyDownCb = () => {}, keyUpCb = () => {}): boolean => {
     4→  const [pressed, setPressed] = useState(false)
     5→  const match = useCallback((event: any) => key.toLowerCase() === event.key.toLowerCase(), [key])
     6→
     7→  const onKeyDown = useCallback(
     8→    (event: KeyboardEvent) => {
     9→      if (match(event)) {
    10→        setPressed(true)
    11→        keyDownCb()
    12→      }
    13→    },
    14→    [keyDownCb, match]
    15→  )
    16→
    17→  const onKeyUp = useCallback(
    18→    (event: KeyboardEvent) => {
    19→      if (match(event)) {
    20→        setPressed(false)
    21→        keyUpCb()
    22→      }
    23→    },
    24→    [keyUpCb, match]
    25→  )
    26→
    27→  useEffect(() => {
    28→    globalThis.addEventListener('keydown', onKeyDown)
    29→    globalThis.addEventListener('keyup', onKeyUp)
    30→
    31→    return () => {
    32→      globalThis.removeEventListener('keydown', onKeyDown)
    33→      globalThis.removeEventListener('keyup', onKeyUp)
    34→    }
    35→  }, [key, onKeyDown, onKeyUp])
    36→
    37→  return pressed
    38→}
    39→

1→import { createSlice, CaseReducer, PayloadAction } from '@reduxjs/toolkit'
     2→
     3→import { CartItem } from '../cart'
     4→
     5→export type OrderState = {
     6→  items: CartItem[]
     7→}
     8→
     9→const saveOrder: CaseReducer<OrderState, PayloadAction<CartItem[]>> = (state, action) => {
    10→  state.items = action.payload
    11→}
    12→
    13→const clearOrder: CaseReducer<OrderState> = (state) => {
    14→  state.items = []
    15→}
    16→
    17→const initialState: OrderState = {
    18→  items: [],
    19→}
    20→
    21→const orderSlice = createSlice({
    22→  name: 'order',
    23→  initialState,
    24→  reducers: {
    25→    saveOrderAction: saveOrder,
    26→    clearOrderAction: clearOrder,
    27→  },
    28→})
    29→
    30→// Extract the action creators object and the reducer
    31→export const { actions, reducer } = orderSlice
    32→
    33→// Extract and export each action creator by name
    34→export const { saveOrderAction, clearOrderAction } = actions
    35→
    36→// Export the reducer, either as a default or named export
    37→export default reducer
    38→

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→import type { Restaurant } from '../types'
     4→
     5→import { api } from './index'
     6→
     7→export const useFetchRestaurants = () => {
     8→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
     9→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    10→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    11→
    12→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    13→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    14→  }, [setRetry])
    15→
    16→  useEffect(() => {
    17→    setStatus('loading')
    18→
    19→    api
    20→      .getRestaurants()
    21→      .then((res) => {
    22→        setStatus('success')
    23→        setRestaurants(res)
    24→      })
    25→      .catch((error) => {
    26→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    27→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    28→      })
    29→  }, [retry])
    30→
    31→  return {
    32→    status,
    33→    restaurants,
    34→    retryRequest,
    35→  }
    36→}
    37→
    38→export const useFetchRestaurantsByCategory = (categoryId?: string) => {
    39→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    40→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    41→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    42→
    43→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    44→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    45→  }, [setRetry])
    46→
    47→  useEffect(() => {
    48→    if (categoryId) {
    49→      setStatus('loading')
    50→
    51→      api
    52→        .getRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)
    53→        .then((res) => {
    54→          setStatus('success')
    55→          setRestaurants(res)
    56→        })
    57→        .catch((error) => {
    58→          const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    59→          setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    60→        })
    61→    }
    62→  }, [categoryId, retry])
    63→
    64→  return {
    65→    status,
    66→    restaurants,
    67→    retryRequest,
    68→  }
    69→}
    70→
    71→export const useFetchRestaurant = (id: string) => {
    72→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    73→  const [restaurant, setRestaurant] = useState<Restaurant>()
    74→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    75→
    76→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    77→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    78→  }, [setRetry])
    79→
    80→  useEffect(() => {
    81→    setStatus('loading')
    82→
    83→    api
    84→      .getRestaurantById(id)
    85→      .then((res) => {
    86→        setStatus('success')
    87→        setRestaurant(res)
    88→      })
    89→      .catch((error) => {
    90→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    91→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    92→      })
    93→  }, [id, retry])
    94→
    95→  return {
    96→    status,
    97→    restaurant,
    98→    retryRequest,
    99→  }
   100→}
   101→

1→import { useRef, useEffect } from 'react'
     2→import * as React from 'react'
     3→import { createPortal } from 'react-dom'
     4→
     5→type Props = {
     6→  selector: string
     7→}
     8→
     9→export const Portal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<Props>> = ({ children, selector }) => {
    10→  const ref = useRef<Element | null>(null)
    11→
    12→  useEffect(() => {
    13→    const element = document.querySelector<Element>(selector)
    14→    if (element) {
    15→      ref.current = element
    16→    }
    17→  }, [selector])
    18→
    19→  return ref.current ? createPortal(children, ref.current) : null
    20→}

1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import { CSSTransition } from 'react-transition-group'
     3→
     4→import { Portal } from '../Portal'
     5→import { useKey } from '../../hooks/useKeyboard'
     6→import { Button } from '../Button'
     7→import { useLockBodyScroll } from '../../hooks/useBodyScrollLock'
     8→
     9→import { ModalContent, TopBar, Backdrop } from './Modal.styles'
    10→
    11→type ModalProps = {
    12→  isOpen: boolean
    13→  onClose: () => void
    14→}
    15→
    16→export const Modal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<ModalProps>> = ({
    17→  children,
    18→  isOpen,
    19→  onClose,
    20→}) => {
    21→  useKey('escape', onClose)
    22→  useLockBodyScroll(isOpen)
    23→  const contentRef = React.useRef(null)
    24→  const backdropRef = React.useRef(null)
    25→
    26→  return (
    27→    <Portal selector="#modal">
    28→      <CSSTransition
    29→        in={isOpen}
    30→        timeout={300}
    31→        classNames="modal"
    32→        unmountOnExit
    33→        nodeRef={contentRef}
    34→      >
    35→        <ModalContent data-testid="modal" ref={contentRef}>
    36→          <TopBar>
    37→            <Button
    38→              data-testid="modal-close-btn"
    39→              onClick={onClose}
    40→              clear
    41→              round
    42→              icon="cross"
    43→              aria-label="close modal"
    44→              iconSize={16}
    45→            />
    46→          </TopBar>
    47→          {children}
    48→        </ModalContent>
    49→      </CSSTransition>
    50→
    51→      <CSSTransition
    52→        in={isOpen}
    53→        timeout={300}
    54→        classNames="backdrop"
    55→        unmountOnExit
    56→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    57→      >
    58→        <Backdrop data-testid="modal-backdrop" ref={backdropRef} onClick={onClose} />
    59→      </CSSTransition>
    60→    </Portal>
    61→  )
    62→}
    63→

     8→  | 'arrow-right'

    10→  | 'cross'
    11→  | 'cart'
    12→  | 'minus'
    13→  | 'plus'

    23→
    24→export const Icon = ({ name, color, size = '1.5rem' }: IconProps) => {
    25→  const { color: themeColor } = useTheme()

    32→    >

    35→  )
    36→}
